Famine [,Gaza] and data science: can we do better? 

 

Katharine Sherratt2, Kevin van Zandvoort1,2, Clara Brigitta2, Ciara McCarthy2, Ciara Judge2, Zeina Jamaluddine1, Francesco Checchi1 [MAY NEED UNRWA AUTHORS] 

 

1 Health in Humanitarian Crises Centre and 2 Centre for Mathematical Modelling of Infectious Diseases 

London School of Hygiene and Tropical Medicine 

 

Introduction 

Israel’s ongoing and past campaigns in the Gaza Strip have consistently instrumentalised civilian food supplies CITE. Since October 2023, Gaza’s population of two million has been, alternately, subjected to near-complete blockades and periods of eased border restrictions. Israeli operations have also minimised Gaza’s own agricultural and fishing production, already well below self-sufficiency CITE. The first semester of the war saw substantial caloric deficits relative to healthy diet requirements CITE, and a more profound shortage of food unfolded in the first half of 2025 after months of total (March to May) and extensive (May onwards) restrictions on trucking and the imposition on Gazans of the inappropriate, inhumane and inconsistent CITEx2 Gaza Humanitarian Foundation distribution system. 

With the exception of Somalia, scientific evidence seems to have played little role in decision-making during past food insecurity crises CITE. In Gaza, successive Integrated Phase Classification (IPC) reports, culminating in projections of famine in parts of the Strip CITE, have however received intense media coverage, and may, behind the scenes, have spurred beneficial levels of diplomatic pressure. While the IPC synthesises different evidence streams, 

[…] 

 

An example: monitoring adult weight 

In August, as current famine conditions were precipitating and humanitarian workers in the Strip began themselves to report running out of food, we initiated a project to remotely monitor weight among United Nations Relief and Works Agency for Palestinian Refugees in the Near East (UNRWA). The project relied on entirely anonymous, opt-in participation by individual staff, with light data collection and automation to update analysis and visualisations as self-readings came in.  

 

[Our approach] 

[more on methods, how it was implemented]  

 

[What we found] 

Limited data so far demonstrate the extent to which even a relatively privileged stratum of Gaza’s society have shed weight relative to pre-war levels (Figure). 

 

[TABLE 1 HERE: sex, age, governorate, role, n dependent children, time of 1st wt measurement, n repeat observations] 

Table 1. Characteristics of a self-selected sample of UNRWA staff in Gaza who provided at least one weight measurement (n = XXX). 

 

 

[FIGURE 1 HERE] 

Figure 1. Mean percent reduction relative to pre-war in weight and body-mass index at the time of first weight measurement among a self-selected sample of UNRWA staff in Gaza (n = XXX), by participant characteristic.  

 

Statistical associations? Model? 

 

Conclusions 

[…] 

1. Identify indicators and data elements that they already produce through their humanitarian assistance work, and that, however unconventional, could be monitored as signals of the nutritional and food security situation over time. These may include morbidity (e.g. admissions or consultations among children and pregnant or lactating women for conditions related to acute malnutrition, such as pneumonia and acute diarrhoea); relapses from malnutrition treatment, the rate of which typically indicates insufficient food at household level; incidence of and fatalities from infections following trauma injuries, common in undernourished individuals; food availability in markets; and any food distribution data. 

2. Adopt an open data approach: data that are aggregated or fully anonymised, and whose visualisation as trends over time and space could help to illustrate conditions inside Gaza, can and should be made available, e.g. on the United Nations data repository, HDX CITE; sharing data openly may not be part of the humanitarian reflex, but the exceptional situation in Gaza requires courage and inventiveness. 

3. Shift from intermittent reports to automated data pipelines and live visualisations, so that the media and key stakeholders become accustomed to predictable, consistent situational awareness. Setting up such pipelines, whereby data, once generated, are automatically picked up and processed into user-friendly graphs, is something we and other academic units can assist with, for free if necessary. The key here is consistency: think weekly data upload and visualisation updates. 

4. Empower Gaza’s civil society to tell its own story, through media of course, but also by helping them collect systematic data that capture their daily experience.
